text: The descriptive claim "tool granularity matters" is already scooped (The Tool Illusion, COLM-2026, web-agent domain). Defensible novelty is reframed to three pillars: a formal-difficulty CSP testbed, a model x granularity interaction, and a predictive cost theory.

bibliography.title: “The Tool Illusion” (COLM 2026)
bibliography.summary: Web-agent study arguing that how tools are framed/granularized changes agent behaviour.
bibliography.relevance: Closest prior work and the one genuinely about granularity: it already makes the descriptive claim that “tool granularity matters,” so that headline is scooped. Our novelty must move past description to a difficulty-controlled cost model.

bibliography.title: Executable Code Actions Elicit Better LLM Agents (CodeAct; Wang et al., ICML 2024)
bibliography.summary: Replaces fixed JSON tool calls with a single expressive action space: executable Python code.
bibliography.relevance: A granularity-adjacent controlled comparison — it varies the *expressiveness* of the action space (consolidated code vs. constrained predefined tools) and finds the more expressive space wins (+up to 20%). But it argues for one extreme (collapse everything into code), not a graded none/fine/medium/coarse sweep, and never ties granularity to a formal-difficulty cost curve.

bibliography.title: Less is More: Optimizing Function Calling for LLM Execution on Edge Devices (Paramanayakam et al., DATE 2025)
bibliography.summary: Selectively reduces the number of tools exposed to the model on edge hardware.
bibliography.relevance: Exhibits a phenomenon close to ours: fewer/coarser tool exposure raises agentic success while cutting execution time up to 70% and power up to 40% — a tool-configuration→cost effect. But it tunes tool *selection/count* for a resource budget, not granularity as an axis swept against problem difficulty.

bibliography.title: ReAct (Yao et al., ICLR 2023)
bibliography.summary: Interleaves chain-of-thought reasoning with tool actions.
bibliography.relevance: Background, not a granularity study: it defines the reason–act loop our agents run in but holds the tool/action granularity fixed.

bibliography.title: Berkeley Function-Calling Leaderboard (BFCL, 2024)
bibliography.summary: Standard evaluation of function-calling accuracy across models.
bibliography.relevance: Motivates the gap: the community measures tool-calling quality but never granularity-as-a-knob on a fixed problem family, which is the controlled testbed we add.
